senses.definition: to look after a child until they reach maturity

examples.x: As a single parent, she worked hard to bring up her son.
The aunt helped bring up her nephew after his parents passed away.
It's essential to bring up a child in an environment that fosters both learning and creativity.

senses.definition: to study thoroughly, especially in preparation for an exam

examples.x: She's swotting up thoroughly on psychology for the research project.
She spent the weekend swotting up thoroughly for the biology test.
I have to swot up on history thoroughly to pass the final exam.
Let's swot up the key concepts thoroughly before the review session.
Students should swot up the formulas extensively for the math competition.

senses.definition: to unintentionally become part of a situation

examples.x: She found herself caught up in a difficult situation at work.
As the conflict escalated, many civilians were caught up in the crossfire.
Without intending to, he got caught up in the middle of the argument.
The tourists were caught up in the local festival happening unexpectedly.
She got caught up in the rush hour traffic and arrived late to the meeting.

senses.definition: to be involved in an activity, often something surprising or unpleasant
to lead a group, team, or organization

examples.x: She was chosen to head up the new marketing campaign.
He's been heading up the initiative to revamp the company's website.
They want someone experienced to head up the project.

senses.definition: to become involved in a situation, especially one that is problematic or unpleasant

examples.x: He found himself mixed up in a conflict he had tried to avoid.
They didn't want to get mixed up in the neighborhood quarrel, but they were dragged into it.
She got mixed up in a legal dispute over a misunderstanding.
He got mixed up in a disagreement between his friends.

senses.definition: to make something bright by means of color or light

examples.x: The Christmas tree lights twinkled brightly, lighting up the living room with a warm, festive glow.
The sunrise painted the sky with hues of orange, pink, and purple, lighting up the world with a new day's promise.
The artist's bold use of color lit up the canvas, creating a vibrant and expressive work of art.

senses.definition: to make a space or environment become brighter and less gloomy, by adding more light sources or using lighter colors and materials

examples.x: Painting the walls a lighter color will lighten up the room and make it feel more spacious.
Adding more lamps and windows will lighten up the space and make it feel less dark and dreary.
Using sheer curtains instead of heavy drapes will lighten up the room and allow more natural light to enter.
Lightening up the living room with brighter paint and new lighting fixtures made it feel more inviting and comfortable.

senses.definition: to be logically consistent

examples.x: The financial figures in the report don't add up; there seems to be an error.
Her explanation of the situation didn't add up, and I had my doubts.
The detective found that the alibi provided by the suspect didn't add up.
His story didn't add up with the known facts of the situation.
When you consider all the facts, it begins to add up and make sense.

senses.definition: (of information) to align or correspond, indicating accuracy or reliability

examples.x: The witness's account of the crime scene matches up with the police report.
The results of the two independent studies match up, suggesting that the conclusion is valid.
The two versions of the story don't match up, leaving room for doubt.
The archaeological artifacts found at the site match up with descriptions from ancient texts, providing valuable insights into the past.

senses.definition: to make something seem more important or noticeable by highlighting it

examples.x: She decided to play up her strengths during the interview.
He plays up his connections to make himself seem more influential.
The media often plays up minor incidents, causing unnecessary panic.
To make the story more engaging, the author played up the main character's internal conflict.

senses.definition: to meet or exceed the established requirements or expectations in terms of quality, performance, or achievement
to block a passage, system, or space, causing a slowdown or complete stop

examples.x: The heavy rain caused leaves and debris to clog up the storm drains, leading to street flooding.
Please avoid putting too much paper in the shredder at once; it tends to clog up and may jam.
Inefficient traffic management can clog up major roads and lead to frustrating delays during rush hours.
The computer's performance started to suffer as unnecessary files began to clog up the hard drive.

senses.definition: to get someone or something ready or prepared for a specific task, event, or challenge

examples.x: The company is gearing up for the product launch with a comprehensive marketing campaign.
We need to gear up for the busy holiday season by hiring additional staff.

senses.definition: to repair something quickly or temporarily

examples.x: After the storm, we had to patch up the roof to prevent any leaks.
She quickly patched up her ripped skirt with a safety pin.
Before the rainy season, it's important to patch up any leaks in the roof.

word_list.pronunciation: ɹˈiːd ˌʌp ˌɑːn ɔːɹ ɐbˌaʊt ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to extensively read on a specific topic to gain more knowledge or understanding

examples.x: To better understand the economic theories discussed in class, I spent hours reading up on the subject in the library.
She read up about ancient civilizations to write a comprehensive history paper for her college course.
To prepare for the job interview, she read up on the company's background and recent achievements.
In his free time, he enjoys reading up about space exploration and the mysteries of the universe.
Before traveling to Japan, I decided to read up on the country's history and culture to make the most of my trip.

senses.definition: to move people or things physically, often in an aggressive and unexpected manner

examples.x: The intense thunderstorm shook up the passengers on the turbulent flight.

senses.definition: to study or learn something quickly, especially in preparation for an exam or an important event

examples.x: She decided to mug up the formulas and equations for the math test.
While preparing for the interview, he is currently mugging up on the company's background and recent achievements.
During finals week, many students are mugging up in the library until late hours.

senses.definition: to notice something that is not immediately obvious

examples.x: As a skilled detective, he could pick up on subtle behavioral cues that indicated when someone was lying.
Sarah was quick to pick up on the tension in the room when she entered.
During the movie, most people didn't pick up on the small clues leading to the big plot twist at the end.
Despite the actor's composed demeanor, keen-eyed fans picked up on the slight tremor in his hands, indicating nervousness.

senses.definition: to memorize information with the intention of sharing it later

examples.x: She liked to store up interesting facts throughout the week to share with her friends on the weekend.
As a journalist, he would constantly store up anecdotes and stories to use in his articles.
The storyteller would store up tales from different cultures to entertain the audience during gatherings.
The tour guide would store up fascinating trivia about each location to captivate the tourists.
Children often store up amusing stories to tell their parents when they come home from school.
